data.title: Installed Building Products: Rising Institutional Pessimism Despite Strong Market Tailwinds
data.description:
Installed Building Products, Inc. (NYSE:IBP) is one week ahead of releasing Q3 2024 earnings results.

Since the stock price surged by 145% during the bull run from October 2023 to March 2024, it has since been moving sideways, showing a slowdown in momentum and an increase in pessimism, particularly among institutional investors, with a 96% increase in the put/call ratio in the last quarter.

Considering the nature of its business, IBP's growth trajectory remains closely tied to the residential construction industry, particularly new single-family home starts.

I believe that market conditions will likely remain favorable in 2025, as further interest rate cuts seem highly likely. This could lead to lower mortgage rates and an increase in buyer confidence.

In this article, I will discuss the rationale behind my Buy rating, including a deep dive into the fundamentals of the company, an overview of insider and institutional activity, and a simple valuation analysis.

For now, I'd like to start with a brief company overview section for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

IBP is an installer of insulation and complementary building products. They operate through a national network of over 250 branch locations across the United States.

They mainly provide installation services for residential new construction, as well as repair, remodeling, and commercial building markets.

Their products include insulation, fireproofing, fire stopping, garage doors, waterproofing, rain gutters, window blinds, and other similar products.

The company operates in one business segment, however the latest 10Q provides a breakdown by end market and product.

Ownership-wise, I am glad to see a 16.5% ownership of common stock among all 14 directors and executive officers.

Price Action

The daily chart below shows a 120% increase in the share price since October 2023.

However, there has been a slowdown this year, with the price trading sideways (although with large swings) since February 2024, with the RSI cooling off in the daily chart.

A comparison with the residential construction industry, the consumer cyclical sector, and the S&P 500 shows IBP outperforming these benchmarks in the last 2 years.

Let's dive deep into the fundamentals and understand what drove this increase in the share price.

Fundamentals

As mentioned in the company overview section, IBP's performance is closely tied to new home construction, especially single-family homes, which make up the majority of their business.

I considered including below a table with the new single-family unit starts per quarter to better understand the bull run in Q4 2023 and Q1 2024.

As you can see, there has been a notable YoY increase in the number of new single-family home starts in both Q4 2023 and Q1 2024.

Therefore, I was not surprised to see revenue and gross profit improving in these two quarters.

Operating income also improved, in the double digits, however, net income declined in Q4 2023, despite the tailwinds in new home construction.

Looking at the figures from the last quarter, the company has maintained its growth trajectory, with revenue, operating income, and net income growing in the mid to high single digits.

Moving on to the balance sheet, the company managed to reduce its net debt in the last year, sitting at $591 million at the end of Q2 2024.

As seen in the table below, cash per share has increased by 50% YoY. Additionally, I am encouraged to see positive tangible book value, which has been improving consistently since Q1 2023, mainly driven by the improvement in cash & equivalents.

As a result of the cash improvements, the liquidity ratios look quite decent.

Ratio Value
Current Ratio 3.08
Quick Ratio 2.59
Cash Ratio 1.08

Debt levels seem quite manageable, with $844.0 million in long-term debt remaining after accounting for current maturities.

Speaking of maturities, the nearest one is in April 2025. This is a note payable of $0.6 million at 5% interest, which is easily manageable considering the $380.3 million cash & equivalents.

Additionally, the company holds a term loan of $494.3 million, refinanced in March 2024 with a new maturity date of March 2031, and $297 million in senior notes due 2028.

Moving on to the cash flow statement, I have to admit that I am encouraged by the double-digit YoY growth in operating and free cash flows.

Aside from the improvements in net income discussed above, there were several non-cash adjustments that impacted in a favorable way operating cash flow in the first half of this year.

Among them, $28.0 million in depreciation and amortization expenses related to property and equipment, $16.3 million in amortization expenses related to the right-of-use assets under operating leases, $21.2 million in amortization of intangibles, and $3.1 million in provision for credit losses.

It seems that the company has been very effective at generating cash from operations, benefiting from the rise in new single-family home starts since the end of 2023.

The outlook for single-family starts in 2025 looks favorable, with Fannie Mae projecting mortgage originations for single-family homes to grow from $1.98 trillion in 2024 to $2.44 trillion in 2025, despite the lack of confidence of residential builders in 2024.

Insider / Activist / Institutional Activity

I remain disappointed with the lack of insider buying activity in the last 2 years. Additionally, in May and March this year, several insiders sold a significant amount of their ownership, including the COO, Wheeler Brad, who sold $273k worth of shares.

Looking at activist activity, there hasn't been anything significant since the beginning of the year.

Institutional investors seem to be more pessimistic, with a 96% increase in the put/call ratio in Q2 2024. Additionally, the number of closed positions increased by 47% in the same quarter.

Considering the valuation ratios of the company (see next section), I am not surprised by the pessimism among institutional investors.

Valuation

A quick look at the chart below shows most valuation ratios trading close to the 5-year maximum.

There is a notable exception for the P/CF ratio, considering the improvement in operating cash flows in the last year.

Let’s compare these ratios to those of their industry peers to see if they are on the higher side.

As a side note, the table above was sorted in descending order by EV/EBITDA. IBP is the 4th most expensive company in the group if we use this metric for comparison.

You can find the results below if we use P/CF as a comparison metric.

In my view, despite most of these ratios trading close to their 5-year maximum, I don't think the company is overvalued when compared to its peers.

Risks

In my opinion, one of the biggest risks to my optimistic outlook is a delay in interest rate cuts, which could keep mortgage costs high and reduce demand for new homes.

Given the company's reliance on new single-family home construction, any slowdown in housing starts would directly impact its revenue.

Additionally, IBP's valuation might appear high compared to its historical levels, making the stock more susceptible to broader market corrections. In my view, if institutional pessimism persists despite interest rate cuts, as signaled by the rising put/call ratio and increased closed positions, the stock could experience further downside.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I hold a Buy rating for IBP, as the company’s strong fundamentals are backed by positive industry trends that are expected to continue into next year.

I am encouraged by the consistent increase (YoY) in new single-family home starts in the last 3 quarters. Additionally, further interest rate cuts could lead to mortgage rates cooling off in 2025, improving buyer's confidence in new single-family units.

Additionally, the company’s balance sheet improvements, including higher cash reserves and manageable debt levels, provide a solid financial foundation if there is a slowdown in new home starts next year.

However, I must consider some risks. The recent slowdown in share price momentum and insider selling activity is concerning. Valuation ratios also appear stretched compared to historical levels, though I find the company fairly priced relative to its peers.

However, I believe the underlying growth potential and cash flow strength outweigh these concerns.

data.title: Snapchat Will Snap Out Of The Rut - Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Snapchat (NYSE:SNAP) will report earnings on October 29th. I'm initiating it with a buy before earnings. Despite the soft revenue guidance for Q3, further exacerbating what I'd call shaky investor confidence with an over 18% dip after the fact, I think the market has priced in the negatives. With the stock hitting a 52-week low of $8.29 on August 7th, I think a rebound is due. The stock's relative strength index is at 40, and it's edging closer to oversold territory. All these parts align to make me believe the worst has already happened, and Snap should see some upside. The disclaimer that goes with my buy rating is that Snap isn't a stock for the faint-hearted. I see more upside in 2HFY24, as the stock's movement proved possible over the last year. If there's one thing Snap has proven year to date, it is its stock's ability to really snap up or plunge after earnings.

I believe Snap has a couple of growth catalysts working in its favor, two of which are making me a buy. The first is management's investments in the Direct response ad platform, which, I think, will show up on top-line in the short term, despite competition from Meta (META) and TikTok. The latter is facing the pressures of being banned in the U.S., and if that happens, I expect it would return some of the migrated users back to Snap.

The other reason why I'm a buy on the long-term is I expect heightened engagement as a result of the company's investments in their augmented reality platform or AR, along with their strides in AI since launching My AI last year and the recent partnership with Google's Gemini on Vertex AI. Thomas Kurian, CEO of Google Cloud, said that Snap was one early leader in the world that helped people communicate...:

now it is at the forefront of using generative AI to build agents that create new value for its community. Gemini models on Vertex AI are now powering millions of multimodal interactions in the Snapchat app every day that allow Snapchatters to better engage with My AI - with built-in accuracy and safety features.

Snap In A Snap: The Stock Performance

On the three-month chart, as seen below, the stock movement shows a negative market sentiment. The stock is down 28.2%, underperforming the S&P 500, which is up 6.3%. Its peer, Pinterest (PINS), is down 19%, but Meta (META) is up 20%, and Reddit (RDDT) is also up 21.4%. I think there is a limited downside from here and growth catalysts that haven't been priced in yet.

The year-to-date chart offers an illustration of a normal day for Snap's stock movement, which proved to be volatile. On the year-to-date chart, Snap is underperforming its peers and the S&P 500. The stock is down 7.4%, against the S&P 500, which is up 11.7%, Meta is up 13.2%, Reddit is up 55.7%, and Pinterest is down 3.7%.

As seen below, the stock plunged over 30% in early February on weak 1Q24 guidance due to the war in the Middle East. Fast-forward to late April, after 1Q24 results beat analysts' expectations, and revenue growth returned to double digits; the stock soared over 23% in extended trading. The last dip was that of two months ago, in early August, as the company reported 2Q24 earnings, and the stock dipped over 20% on weak 3Q24 guidance. With all said and done, I think the stock has an upside ahead after the company guides higher than investor expectations on the next earnings report with already low market expectations. I think that's going to happen for two main reasons, which I'll discuss in a second.

DR Ad Growth To The Rescue

DR advertising revenue increased 16% year-over-year in Q2, which is in line with the 17% in Q1, regardless of the tough comparison. The nature of brand businesses is very volatile, and marketing investments can be limited according to the ongoing economic situation. This can be seen in Q2 North America revenue that grew as little as 12% year-over-year due to the "impact of weaker brand-oriented demand" that's mainly concentrated in the region. Despite that, DR was a bright spot in the region, backed by strong growth in the SMB customer segment, which I'll get to in a second. Now, back to the tougher economic environment, it affected certain marketing investments in verticals where the company has had really good performance in direct response. An example of that is last quarter's restaurants vertical, which witnessed a tough environment in certain cases, but according to management, since DR is based on performance,

that's a category that continues to grow for us on the DR side in the current quarter because we've got great performance for those advertisers, and they've been able to continue to invest and grow their business on the back of that.

Due to the macro volatility I mentioned earlier, management is now more focused on investing to counter that volatility from all sides, and I'm optimistic about that. The company is seeing good momentum on its 7-0 Pixel Purchase Optimizations. On the advertisers' side, there has been a great benefit of the 7-0 optimization for app installation and app purchases, which showed good results during testing last quarter. CAP adoption is up 300% year-over-year, further indicating improved performance. That, along with the increase in total active advertisers to more than double year-over-year last quarter, should reflect in continued growth in DR advertising revenue in 2H24.

While we're on the SMB segment, management is focusing a big chunk of investments in the coming year on this segment. I share management's sentiment and think it "leverages the strength of our scale with our community and also our ability on the product and engineering side to drive results for advertisers." I think this will help with long-term growth and give the company a diversified revenue stream.

Valuation

Snap has a market cap of 17.41 billion and an enterprise value of 18.30 billion. Market sentiment on the stock is generally cautious, with over 72% of Street Analysts giving it a hold rating. Around 4.5% of Street analysts give the stock a strong buy, and ~16% give it a buy. Only 6.8% of analysts give the stock a sell.

The PT median and mean both notice a downward trend, reflecting investor caution and a dash of pessimism about the stock. The PT median was at $16 in late July and went down to $13 in August. The downward trajectory continued into September at $12 and is currently maintaining that. The mean PT was around $15 in July and was also down to $12.9 in August. It continued downwards to $12.6 in September, currently at $12.4. According to data from Refinitiv, Snap's EV/Sales (TTM) is 3.56, and its EV/Sales is 3.31, significantly lower than Meta's EV/Sales (TTM) at 9.7 and EV/Sales at 8.97.

Free cash flow was negative $73 million last quarter, down 38% from a year ago quarter, still reflecting the seasonality of lower revenue in Q1. I expect the numbers to improve now that headwinds from seasonality are behind us and management is focusing on balanced investments to sustain a positive cash flow. Net loss saw an improvement in Q2, at $249 million compared to the net loss of $377 million in a year ago quarter. I think the headcount reduction related to restructuring recently reflected positively on expenses. I believe such tactical reduction in expenses positions the company for continued profit down the line, accompanied by strategic investments. Speaking of which, for Q3 investment plans, management is prioritizing investments carefully to "deliver against the cost plans we have set out for our business, while investing prudently to deliver for our community and our partners."

What's Next: Annual Snap Partner Summit

AR

Last quarter, the number of users sharing AR Lens experiences increased by 12% year-over-year due to AR experience optimizations and AI Lenses' popularity. A clear indication is the ML Scribble World Lens, which was viewed over 1 billion times during the same quarter. At the annual partner summit event in mid-September, management revealed the fifth generation of its AR glasses, along with enhancements that add to its competitive edge. With creator-driven content taking a more significant chunk of attention for management, the number of creators "posting publicly has more than tripled over the past year," with over "15 billion interactions between creators and their fans." In his remarks, the company's CEO Evan Spiegel discussed VR sets in comparison to AR sets, in a way throwing shade at rivals Meta and Apple (AAPL). He said:

VR headsets…feel like you've got a laptop taped to your face, and they're isolating, they make people feel motion sick, they're heavy, they're uncomfortable. But AR glasses have see-through lenses. They allow you to share experiences together with your friends in the real world, and they're lightweight and wearable.

AI

At the summit, the company also introduced an AI-generated tool to creators in an effort to enhance its AI and machine learning capabilities for a better user experience. After unveiling My AI last year, a GPT-3 chatbot for Snapchat+ subscribers. According to Snapchat's vice president of products, Ceci Mourkogiannis, the new tool will help creators "make engaging videos with AI from a simple text, or soon, image prompt." I believe Snap is one step ahead of Meta and ByteDance's TikTok, which are yet to have their own AI-generated video capabilities.

data.title: AGCO Corporation - Improving Returns Came From Efficiency Improvements
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the past decade, AGCO Corporation (NYSE:AGCO) has managed to improve its returns. This came from improving operating margins, as reflected by better gross profit margins and lower SGA margins. This is an important edge as the agricultural machinery market is mature with a single-digit revenue growth rate. Profits growth will have to come from improving efficiencies.

The company is also financially sound and a good cash generator, which will mitigate the debt it took to acquire Trimble. I also found that there is more than a 30% margin of safety at the current market price. This is an investment opportunity.

Background

AGCO was founded in 1990 as a manufacturer and distributor of farm equipment in North America. The company has since grown to be “a global leader in the design, manufacture, and distribution of agricultural machinery and precision agriculture technology.”

  • In 2023, North America only accounted for about ¼ of the total revenue. You can see from the right part of Chart 1 that currently Europe/Middle is the biggest revenue contributor.
  • In terms of products, the company's main revenue came from tractors. In 2023, sales of tractors accounted for about 60% of the total revenue. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

Note to Chart 1: The 2014 Europe/Middle East revenue included those from Africa.

Over the past few months, there were 2 important changes to the business direction:

  • In April 2024, the company completed the USD 2 billion joint venture with Trimble Inc. “to better serve farmers with factory fit and aftermarket applications in the mixed fleet precision agriculture market.”
  • In July 2024, the company “entered into a definitive agreement to sell the majority of its Grain & Protein business for USD 700 million.

The company stated that the sale of the Grain & Protein business “does not represent a strategic shift that will have a major effect on the consolidated results of operations.”

Nevertheless, taken together with the Trimble joint venture, I see this as a sign that the company will continue to focus on the agricultural machinery sector.

I do not see AGCO becoming a tech company but rather one that taps into digital tech to better serve the farmers. This is analogous to car companies using digital technology to enhance the user experience. They want to sell more cars. I do not think that car companies are going the way of Uber.

In this context, the global tractors and agricultural machinery market is a mature one with projected single-digit growth.

“The global agricultural tractors market demand was 3,200 thousand units in 2023… is expected to reach around 5,841 thousand units by 2033, expanding at a CAGR of 6.2% from 2024 to 2033.” Precedence Research

“The global agriculture tractor market size was valued at 2.05 million units in 2023 and is expected to reach 2.56 million units by 2029, growing at a CAGR of 3.76% during the forecast period” Arizton.

“Global demand for agricultural equipment is projected to advance 3.6% per year to $216 billion in 2028.” Freedonia Group

“The global demand for the agricultural equipment market is projected to increase at a CAGR of 4.2% during the forecast period between 2023 and 2033” Future Market Insights.

Apart from single-digit growth, there was also a significant increase in the Producer Price Index for agricultural machinery over the past few years. Refer to the left part of Chart 2.

Operating trends

Given the single-digit projected growth rates, you should not be surprised to find that over the past decade, AGCO’s revenue grew at 4.5% CAGR.

Given the single-digit growth in market demand, I would deduce that AGCO maintained its market share.

As can be seen from the left part of Chart 3, the bulk of the growth seemed to be from 2021 onwards. The company attributed this to “improved market demand and favorable pricing impacts.”

I think this is in line with the increase in the Producer Price Index shown in Chart 2. The result was a significant improvement in profits from 2020 to 2023. Refer to the left part of Chart 3.

The returns followed the profit performance as illustrated in the right part of Chart 3. You can see the significant jump from 2020 for the ROIC and ROE. Over the past decade,

  • ROIC averaged 12% compared to the current WACC of 8%.
  • ROE averaged 13% compared to the current cost of equity of 10%

With both these returns greater than the respective cost of funds, they suggest that shareholders’ value was created.

Efficiencies

Over the past 12 years, there was also an improvement in gross profitability (gross profits/total assets). This averaged 31% for 2022/23 compared to 26% for 2014/15. Refer to the left part of Chart 3.

In addition, there was also a better gross profit margin and a decline in the Selling, General, and Administration (SGA) margin. This demonstrates good cost control. There was also an improvement in the contribution margin. Refer to Chart 4.

Note to Op Profit Profile. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization and Others.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

To see whether the improvement in efficiency was broad-based, I looked at the performance of several operating and capital efficiencies metrics. I would conclude that while there was an improvement in the operating efficiency, I could not say the same thing for capital efficiency.

  • Chart 5, left part - Operating efficiency index. Except for inventory turnover that declined, all the other 3 metrics showed improvements.
  • Chart 5, right part - Capital efficiency index. There was only an improvement in the Reinvestment margin.

Peer comparison

I compared AGCO with several global agricultural machinery companies, as listed in Table 1. Apart from its recent top position in the return on capital, I would rate AGCO's performance as average compared to its peers.

You can see that AGCO is among the smaller ones in the peer group in terms of revenue. However, it delivered about average revenue growth from 2014 to 2023. I think this is consistent with my deduction that AGCO managed to maintain its market share over the past decade.

AGCO had an improving return on capital and has become the best among its peers since 2020. However, it was among the bottom performers when it came to EBIT margin. Refer to Chart 6.

In the context of the Levered Free Cash Flow Margin and EPS, I would rate AGCO's performance as average. Refer to Chart 7.

Financial position

I would rate AGCO financially sound based on the following.

From 2014 to 2023, it delivered positive cash flow from operations every year. During this period, it generated USD 6.7 billion cash flow from operations, compared to its USD 4.8 billion net income. This is a very good cash conversion ratio.

It had USD 657 million in cash and short-term investments as of the end of June 2024. This was about 5% of its total assets.

Over the past 12 years, it had a Reinvestment rate of 34%. Excluding the acquisitions, it had an average Reinvestment rate of 16%. Reinvestment rate = Reinvestment / NOPAT. This meant that a big part of the NOPAT could be returned to shareholders.

It had a good capital allocation plan as shown in Table 2. The cash flow from operations was enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions. Excess was returned to shareholders as dividends and share buybacks.

While it had a debt-capital ratio of 47% as of Jun 2024, this has increased from its pre-2024 high of 37% to fund the Trimble joint venture.

According to the Damodaran Jan 2024 data set, the debt-capital ratio for the machinery sector was 14%. I would not worry too much about AGCO's debt level given its historical cash generation ability as illustrated in Table 2.

Valuation

I adopted the following picture in valuing AGCO.

This is a mature company in a mature sector. I do not expect the Trimble joint venture to lead to significant revenue growth. In its announcement on the joint venture, Trimble stated that its “precision agriculture business…is expected to generate fiscal 2023 revenues of approximately $535 million.” In this context:

  • I will value AGCO based on a perpetual revenue growth rate of 4%.
  • I will use the company’s 2024 revenue outlook as the base revenue. This is 13% lower than the 2023 revenue.

Product prices were high over the past few years, boosting margins and efficiencies. On the conservative side, I will use the past 10 years' margin and efficiencies as the base.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 133 per share compared to its market price of USD 99 per share (22 Oct 2024). There is more than 30% margin of safety.

This margin of safety is supported by the Acquirer’s Multiple and Free Cash Flow yield, as shown in Table 3.

Notes to Table 3:

a) EV / (average 3 years Op profit + Depreciation).

b) 2014 to 2023 Average Free cash flow / market price.

Valuation model

I used a single-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) model where the EBIT was determined based on the following:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) - Reinvestment

EBIT = Revenue X contribution margin – Fixed cost. This was based on the operating model as shown in the right part of Chart 4.

The Reinvestment was derived from the historical Reinvestment margin.

Value of firm = FCFF X (1+g) / (WACC – g).

Value of equity = Value of firm + cash + Other investments – Debt – Minority interests + excess Total capital employed

Table 4 illustrates the calculation.

The WACC was determined from the first page results of a Google search for “AGCO WACC” as illustrated in Table 5.

Risks and limitations

I think the key valuation risk relates to Chart 2. Product prices have been high over the past few years relative to pre-2020 by about 40% to 50%.

I am not sure whether the current prices will hold. In my valuation, I used the 2024 revenue projection by the company in its Q2 2024 presentation. This is lower than the 2023 revenue by about 13%. If this was further reduced by 6%, I would get the market price of the stock.

I do not have a crystal ball on how the long-term product price will be. To mitigate this, I carried out a Monte Carlo simulation of the valuation that focus on the revenue downside. The simulation used random samples of the following variables, assuming that they follow a triangular distribution.

  • Revenue. The revenue would range from the base value to 20% lower.
  • Efficiencies. The contribution margin, capital turnover, and Reinvestment margin would range from – 10% to + 10% of the base values.
  • WACC. This would range from 7% to 8.8% as per the range shown in Table 5.

The results based on 2,000 runs are shown in Chart 8. There is a 50% chance that the intrinsic value would be higher than the market price. Note that this is an analysis skewed towards lower product prices.

In other words, assuming that revenue in the long run would never be higher than the Jun 2024 projection, there is a 1-to-1 odds that the intrinsic value is greater than the market price. I would have thought that this is a good downside protection picture.

Conclusion

I would consider AGCO fundamentally sound.

  • It maintained its market share over the past decade. Its joint venture with Trimble will ensure that it can enhance the solutions it provides to its customers.
  • It managed to improve its profits, which in turn led to improving returns. It had the best returns over the past few years compared to its global peers.
  • It is financially sound with a good cash generation track record.
  • There are signs of improving operating efficiencies, although there is some doubt about improving capital efficiencies.

A conservative valuation shows that there is more than a 30% margin of safety. Furthermore, a Monte Carlo simulation that focuses on revenue downside shows a 1-to-1 odds that the intrinsic value would be greater than the market price.

You can understand why I see this as an investment opportunity.

data.title: Tesla, Inc. 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Tesla, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: Rivian: I Was Early And Wrong (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

The first time I wrote about Rivian Automotive, Inc. (NASDAQ:RIVN) stock here on Seeking Alpha was in November 2021 - that was an IPO analysis where I concluded that the stock had been overvalued to a great extent at the very beginning of its public journey. I held my "Sell" rating until June 2023 when I upgraded RIVN to "Hold", thinking that the company's situation was improving at the time as its margins were showing clear signs of growth; nevertheless, I couldn't assign a "Buy" rating due to its high valuation and strong competition risk. But on July 02, 2024, I decided to upgrade my rating to "Buy" as I considered Rivian's partnership with Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) as a major catalyst that should provide strong backing for Rivian's balance sheet and growth prospects. The stock surged over 26% since that call but then quickly gave up all the gains and even managed to lose almost 1/3 of its market cap since the beginning of July:

Now I look at my decision and realize that perhaps I was too early - recent corporate developments and financials clearly show that I was too optimistic. I still think RIVN stock could go up - now probably for technical reasons - but I have decided to revert to my "Hold" rating today. I see Rivian's longer-term challenges outweighing any potential upside - the risks buyers are taking today are too great to ignore.

Why Do I Think So?

In Q2 FY2024, Rivian's $1.2 billion in sales came from deliveries of 13,790 cars, even as the company experienced production disruption due to plant shutdowns for retooling upgrades. As the management explained on the call, this downtime was required "to roll new technologies and material cost-reduction improvements into the R1 vehicle platform that should enhance production performance and cost savings." The retooling is one element in Rivian's wider plan to improve profitability through vertical integration and cost reduction as they continue to scale second-generation R1 vehicles and build out the R2 platform.

To date, marginality remains a huge problem for RIVN - the improvements on this front I expected previously haven't materialized so far. The overall gross profit loss in Q2 was $451 million, a gross profit loss per vehicle of around $33,000 (including $15,000 of depreciation and amortization and $1,200 in stock-based compensation per vehicle). Rivian also spent $2,400/vehicle on cost of revenue efficiency initiatives that won't be part of the future cost equation. On an unadjusted basis, the company missed consensus expectations in terms of earnings, even though we saw a positive surprise in terms of sales and adjusted EPS. Above all, Wall Street analysts have lowered their estimates for the coming quarter (the publication of Q3 FY2024 results is set for November 7, 2024).

Rivian's management, however, said they expect modest positive gross profit for Q4 2024 and for FY2025, due to "material cost reductions, fixed cost leverage, and higher revenue per unit delivered." On the other hand, we found out a few days after the Q2 report came out that the firm is temporarily halting production of its electric commercial van for Amazon (AMZN) because of a parts shortage (but again, they expect to recover the missed production without affecting the R1 pickup and SUV models).

In addition to that, there was a fire at Rivian's Illinois plant that damaged several vehicles in a parking lot (but the assembly plant itself was unaffected). Also, the management stated in early October that RIVN produced 13,157 vehicles and delivered 10,018 in Q3, which was slightly below analyst expectations. These figures came due to "a production disruption from a component shortage affecting both the R1 and RCV platforms", according to the statement. Consequently, Rivian adjusted its annual production guidance to 47,000-49,000 vehicles while maintaining its delivery outlook of 50,500-52,000 vehicles, with Q3 financial results expected on November 7, as I mentioned above already.

In light of all this, I think Rivian's outlook from the Q2 earnings call and management's commentary at the time are probably no longer relevant to the market - now the uncertainty is driving the stock to the lows it had before the surge due to the VW deal.

By the way, Rivian's book grew through an initial $1 billion contribution from Volkswagen, followed by up to $4 billion in capital raisings. This alliance should develop next-generation electrical design and software architecture, giving Rivian the capital to fund R2 ramp-up and lead to positive free cash flow eventually (something I still believe in). The partnership is also expected to result in material savings and operational efficiencies, so at some point, we should see margin expansion.

The company is also planning to lower R1 costs after 2024 through a reduction in material and conversion costs, the leverage of supplier negotiations, and the Volkswagen joint venture. The new R1 platform with the new Ascent motor system, and the second-generation R1 platform are key components of Rivian's approach to making the vehicle perform better at lower production costs.

The problem with Rivian's prospects is that I think they're almost always deferred. Three months ago, we saw a forecast that RIVN would break even on earnings per share (on an adjusted basis) at the end of FY2029 - now the nearest positive year for the company is set for FY2030, with the estimated bottom line number of about 52% lower than a quarter ago. Moreover, the projected figure makes Rivian quite expensive in terms of the implied P/E ratio (17.3x), considering that we're talking about FY2030 here.

The focus on "cost savings, efficiency, and innovation" at the company sounds good indeed, but I think Rivian has to grow way more aggressively to justify its prospective valuation. The deal with VW brought a real boost to RIVN's balance sheet liquidity - the cash on the balance sheet now exceeds 58% of RIVN's market cap, which is hard to believe. But given that the firm keeps burning more and more cash (almost -$4.9 billion in CFO last year, -$4 billion on a TTM basis), this pile of cash should mean little to shareholders, in my opinion.

More important should be the answer to the question: "How quickly will the company stop burning cash and at least break even on an adjusted basis?" And this is where I see real problems, as competition in the EV industry is growing by leaps and bounds every year.

The Bottom Line

Overall, I think we can call Rivian a hostage to market conditions today. The company's products have good reviews as far as I see it, but that may not be enough to somehow increase production at the current car prices. Investors can only hope for contracts with giants like Amazon, but even here there are difficulties, as we could see from the recent news about the shortage of some parts for van production.

However, Wall Street has become much more skeptical in the last 3 months and has once again pushed back its break-even forecast by a year. I remember there were times when the market expected Rivian to break even in 2027 to 2028, but that seems almost unimaginable today. I fear that with the current input data, Rivian has little chance of surprising with something very positive in early November when the third quarter results are announced. However, I can't rule out such a possibility: perhaps we will see a nice bounce from today's prices - the technicals on a weekly basis suggest strong support at current levels.

On the other hand, I can't keep my bullish stance on technicals alone when fundamentals don't support it. Hence, my downgrade to "Hold" today.

Thank you for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Silver Hits 12-Year Highs But Is Still Cheap
data.description:
Silver has recently hit multi-year highs. But it is still very cheap compared to the 2011 and 1980 highs. Despite gold’s and silver’s remarkable rallies, these still remain worth considering. Gold has recently touched a high of $1750. But silver seems to have greater upside potential than gold due to its undervaluation and the gray metal’s volatility. As I am writing this, silver is trading at a price of $34, but more is yet to come, it seems.

My previous article on silver

As mentioned in my previous analysis of silver, the gray shiny metal is often overlooked versus gold. Still, silver has greater potential due to its volatility and growth during crises and periods of excessive money printing.

I also wrote about silver’s underperformance relative to gold and the fact that silver was a great investment opportunity. I also praised silver as a unique asset at a time of uncertainty.

I am now updating my previous analysis because silver has substantially appreciated since the publication of the article “Silver, The Small And Unloved Brother Of Gold, Is A Growth Star”. Moreover, I would like to briefly discuss the ways to profit from the gray metal’s brilliant performance because I still think silver prices have further room to rise.

Why should silver prices rise further?

Below there is a graph showing silver’s price gains since 1975. As of the time of writing, silver is trading at a price of around $34 per ounce. Below is a diagram unadjusted for inflation. We all understand that $50 in 2011 is worth more than $50 today. The $35 high reached in 1970-1980 is also much higher than the $34 now.

But if we just extend the trend line on the graph, we will see that there are further gains to be made.

It seems to me that if we just extend the graph, in about 10 years, silver could cost as much as $60. But this is just a trend analysis. I will not go into many details on the next price target for silver, though. Instead, I will talk to you about the current fundamental factors driving the gray metal’s prices.

As I have mentioned many times before, the demand for silver is due to industrial uses and also investors choosing the metal as an alternative to gold. That is why when the interest rates and the dollar’s value decrease, the prices of both gold and silver rise. Silver is used in manufacturing solar batteries, semiconductor production, the making of touchscreens, and batteries used in electronics. A while ago, I wrote an article titled “Silver may surge to $100 thanks to AI”. In this article, I explained how artificial intelligence technologies could push the demand for silver upwards. But it is not because of AI or any other industrial uses that the silver prices are surging right now, it seems.

As I have mentioned before, gold has been rising in value as well. So, both metals are rising. In my view, this is due to the BRICS (Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa) summit that is now being held in Russia. Many analysts accept the possibility that BRICS nations could establish a currency backed by gold to rival the US dollar. Due to the BRICS alliance's expansion, there are discussions that the dollar’s importance will decrease. As an alternative to the USD, the countries could establish a currency, possibly a digital one, backed by gold as a secure, inflation-resistant asset, which has always played a crucial role in the world’s financial history. BRICS nations’ central banks are increasing their gold reserves, thus raising gold prices amid a potential de-dollarization shift.

You might be wondering why silver is appreciating as well. After all, no one wants to have a currency backed by silver. Well, to start with, if an alternative to the USD as a medium of exchange gets established, the demand for the US dollar will decrease. Therefore, the prices of precious metals nominated in US dollars should rise simply due to the dollar’s depreciation. Meanwhile, silver is often seen as a safe-haven asset and a hedge against inflation, which could hedge or even replace the US dollar when the credibility of the USD is compromised.

Some market experts, including David Morgan, publisher of The Morgan Report and creator of Silver-Investor.com, say that silver is more convenient than gold as a medium of exchange and a store of value. Silver is much cheaper than gold, which makes it more convenient to transport and use. According to Mr. Morgan, “In an absolute crisis, silver would be the monetary metal of choice. Its value per unit is a lot less than gold”. The gray metal has been a synonym of money throughout history. In other words, silver has traditionally been used as money, more often than gold ever has.

Silver’s undervaluation

There is quite a popular way to judge if silver is overvalued or not. There is the gold-to-silver ratio. The graph below shows that the ratio is off its highs reached between 2020 and 2021. However, it is still very high, lingering around the 80 mark, over and above the 50-60 average level.

Gold-to-silver ratio

Dow-to-silver ratio

The Dow-to-silver ratio is close to its all-time highs. The only time when the stock market was so expensive relative to the undervalued silver was in the 1990s -2000s during the so-called “Dot.com bubble”. At the time, high-tech companies were highly overvalued, which kept the stock indices elevated. Silver, meanwhile, only traded for a price between $4 and $6 during the period.

Silver price history

After the lows, reached in the 1990s-2000s, silver prices started surging. In 2011, the commodity prices touched a multi-year high of $48.60.

Ways to profit from silver’s growth

There are various ways to profit from silver’s growth. As I have written in my previous articles before, my recommended way of investing is buying the physical metal itself, either in the form of bars or coins. However, there are also drawbacks to investing that way. The most obvious one is the high storage costs. It is also possible to buy individual companies’ shares. At the same time, getting every single company’s accounting and other fundamentals right requires some substantial knowledge and experience. Those investors willing to take advantage of silver’s strong growth but unwilling to risk choosing individual companies or paying for the physical metals’ storage can still invest in ETFs.

The largest and most popular silver ETF in the US is iShares Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:SLV). From all the silver ETFs, it has the largest amount of assets under management. Its shares’ trading volumes are also the highest. The management fee is only 0.50% per year, which is quite moderate.

But the ETF’s most important advantage is the fact its shares move in direct correlation with silver prices, as can be seen from the graph below.

Holding a share in SLV ETF is not free, but its management costs might be lower than storing physical metal.

Risks to the bullish case on silver

The risks to my bullish position on silver are obvious. The first and foremost one is that of higher rates for longer. If the US unemployment rate does not rise by much, the inflation rate will rise or at least stay unchanged, then there is a risk the Fed may be too slow on their rate cuts. This will be bearish for precious metals in the short-to-medium term. However, the Fed may provoke a recession and eventually be forced to increase the money-printing rate.

The economic slowdown may also be a piece of bad news for industrial demand for silver but good news for investment demand for the gray metal.

Finally, there is a risk that silver’s fundamentals may simply be ignored by the general public. A substantial share of investors prefer to buy high-growth assets such as high-tech companies’ stocks. This may mean the demand for silver as an investment class may be limited. However, this does not seem to be the case right now. As I have mentioned at the beginning of this article, the trend looks very strong for silver.

Conclusion

Despite its remarkable growth, silver is still not as expensive as it could be. I am personally strongly against buying assets at their multi-year highs. But it still seems to me that the gray metal’s rally is far from over. Even though I am in favor of buying physical metals, buying SLV ETF is also a fair alternative for investors willing to take advantage of the gray metal’s miraculous rally.

data.title: Verizon Q3: Buy The Drop
data.description:
Verizon (NYSE:VZ) reported mixed results for its third fiscal quarter on Tuesday, which included a slight earnings beat and weaker-than-expected revenue. However, the telecommunications company benefited from strong subscriber momentum in its core broadband business and Verizon earned a ton of free cash flow in the third quarter as well... leading to a dividend coverage ratio well in excess of 200%. Since shares of the telecom are still very reasonably valued, trade at an earnings yield of 19% and the payout is growing, I believe Verizon represents top value for income investors on the drop.

Previous rating

Strong momentum in the broadband business, and the potential for accelerating down payments of the company's net debt were two reasons why I recommended shares of the telecom to dividend investors in July: Ignore The Noise, Buy The Dip. Verizon continued to see a healthy amount of new net additions in the broadband segment and although the telecom missed revenue targets for the third-quarter, Verizon delivered very strong free cash flow. The very comfortable dividend coverage ratio and the fact that the dividend is growing are two main reasons why investors may want to buy the drop here.

Strong subscriber momentum, easy Q3'24 beat

Verizon reported mixed results for its third-quarter: the telecom had adjusted earnings of $1.19 per share, which beat the consensus estimate of $0.01 per-share. While earnings beat, the telecom missed Q3'24 top-line estimates by $90M, chiefly due to weakness in its phone segment. The company's core broadband business, however, continued to do very well.

Verizon continued to make gains in the broadband business in the September quarter: in Q3'24 the telecom added a total of 389,000 subscribers in the broadband business which included fixed wireless net additions of 363,000 subscribers. While broadband net adds fell 10% year-over-year, the telecom extended a previous streak lasting eight quarters of net additions exceeding 375k.

Verizon's top line remained flat year-over-year, in part because of a slow phone upgrade cycle, but wireless service revenues kept growing: they went up 2.7% year-over-year and Verizon confirmed its full-year outlook of 2.0-3.5% wireless service revenue growth.

The most important takeaway from Verizon's Q3'24 earnings sheet, besides an unchanged outlook for wireless service revenue growth and strong subscriber net additions, was its strong free cash flow. Verizon generated $6.0B in free cash flow in the third fiscal quarter and paid a total of $2.8B in dividends. This calculates to a dividend coverage ratio of 214% and renders the dividend very secure for investors, in my opinion. On a 9-month basis, the dividend coverage was 173%, lower than in Q3'24, but still more than sufficient to pay shareholders a growing dividend.

While AT&T also pays a dividend, Verizon is the one telecom that is growing its dividend payout. Verizon last raised its dividend by 2% to $0.6775 per-share. AT&T's dividend record is blemished, in part because the telecom dumped its WarnerMedia business unit two years ago... which led to a dividend reset. From a dividend growth perspective, Verizon is clearly the better choice for dividend investors.

Leverage needs to be addressed

Verizon owes a considerable amount of debt, but the telecom did not yet make much progress in terms of lowering its financial obligations. At the end of the September quarter, Verizon had long-term debt of $128.9B, meaning its debt actually went up $2.9B quarter-over-quarter. As I said in my last work on AT&T (T) -- AT&T: Moment Of Truth -- debt repayments are a potential catalyst for telecoms to see higher valuations in the future. This is because telecoms have a lot of financial debt that costs them a ton of interest each quarter -- Verizon has recurring quarterly interest expenses of about $1.7B -- so reducing this debt could be a lever for free cash flow growth going forward.

Verizon's valuation

Shares of Verizon dropped 5% after Q3'24 earnings, which I believe creates a new engagement opportunity for dividend investors. Verizon is currently valued at a price-to-earnings ratio of 8.8X, which implies an earnings yield of 11.4%. AT&T, Verizon's fiercest rival in the U.S. telecom market, is trading at a forward (FY 2025) price-to-earnings ratio of 9.4X... implying an earnings yield of 10.6%.

I believe both telecoms could trade at 10-11X P/E ratios under the condition that they make progress repaying more debt and reducing their leverage profiles. A 10-11X P/E results in a fair value range of $47 to $52 per share, implying up to 25% upside revaluation potential. AT&T, as I indicated in my last work, has made more considerable progress here lately and reduced its net debt by billions of dollars. On the other hand, Verizon saw its long-term debt tick up in Q3'24, but the telecom is growing its dividend... leading to a better dividend growth and value proposition.

Risks with Verizon

Verizon is a slow-growth telecom investment and investors, given the maturity of the U.S. telecom market, shouldn't expect much in terms of revenue or EPS growth. Most likely, Verizon is going to see low single-digit growth in its top-line going forward and slightly stronger growth in its wireless services business. What would change my mind about Verizon is if the telecom were to see no significant achievements (that is, reductions) in its net financial debt going forward or if the company saw a decline in its subscriber growth rates in its broadband business.

Closing thoughts

Verizon submitted a fairly decent earnings sheet for the third quarter that showed both better-than-expected earnings and weaker revenues, leading to an exaggerated 5% share price drop. Verizon added a solid 389k new subscribers, net, to its broadband business in the September quarter, and the telecom easily supported its dividend with free cash flow. Shares of Verizon are trading at a very attractive earnings yield of 11% which is the second-most important reason why I recommend the telecom (the first being its consistent customer acquisition momentum in broadband). Further, Verizon is growing its dividend, which is something that makes the telecom stand out from AT&T. I believe investors are dealing with a new engagement opportunity after the 5% drop on Tuesday, and I see no major reason why I would want to deviate from a buy recommendation.

data.title: General Motors: Inflection Point
data.description:
Shares of General Motors (NYSE:GM) surged 10% on Tuesday after the automaker reported better than the expected results for the third fiscal quarter. For the third time this year, General Motors raised its full-year outlook in terms of EBIT and free cash flow, and the company saw some solid momentum in terms of electric vehicle deliveries in the third-quarter as well. EV sales surged 60% compared to the year-earlier period, and the company maintains its outlook to produce and wholesale 200k electric vehicles this year. General Motors continues to trade at a very attractive valuation, despite shares just hitting new 1-year highs, and I see an overall favorable risk profile for investors.

Previous rating

I aggressively recommended shares of General Motors as a strong buy after the automaker disappointed with its second-quarter earnings sheet in July: Buy The Drop, High Safety Margin. The main reason for my rating and my strong buy recommendation was that General Motors raised its full-year outlook for adjusted EBIT and saw positive free cash flow momentum as well. General Motors raised its FY 2024 guidance again and confirmed its EV goals. With EV sales surging, General Motors is set to experience an inflection point in its business.

General Motors crushes earnings, EV business is nearing inflection point

The automaker beat expectations easily on the top and the bottom line for Q3’24: General Motors had adjusted earnings of $2.96 per-share, which beat the consensus estimate by a massive $0.56 per-share. The top line came in at $48.8B, beating the average prediction by an impressive $4.4B, on strong ICE and electric vehicle sales.

The third-quarter was a strong quarter for the auto company: General Motors generated $48.8B in net revenue in Q3’24, showing 11% year-over-year growth, which crushed consensus estimates. General Motors' net income was $3.1B and earnings were supported by a strong sales environment: General Motors' North America especially saw a robust performance, with the geography benefiting from 14% adjusted EBIT growth on higher wholesale volumes.

While General Motors' ICE sales are booming as well, the auto company is starting to crush it in electric vehicle sales as well. The automaker reported that it sold 32,095 electric vehicles in the third-quarter, showing an impressive 60% year-over-year growth rate as well as 46% quarter-over-quarter growth.

This momentum has staying power, in my opinion, as the company is gearing up to launch new electric vehicle models in FY 2025 which could boost the segment's overall performance. Electric vehicles like the Cadillac Escalade IQ and the Chevrolet Equinox EV have the potential to sustain GM's growth in the EV segment. The automaker also confirmed that it was targeting to build and wholesale 200k GM-branded electric-vehicles this year in North America.

With momentum building in the EV segment, General Motors could achieve an important inflection point in its business next year. GM said that it could be profitable on a 200k annual production volume (based off of contribution margin) which is the target set out for this year. In FY 2025, General Motors could therefore really see a boost in EV segment profitability, which may be a catalyst for shares to go into a new up-leg.

Revised guidance for FY 2024

General Motors raised its guidance for the third time this year and now projects adjusted EBIT of $14-15B (+$1B at the low-end of guidance) and free cash flow of $12.5-13.5B, compared to a previous guidance of $9.5-11.5B. General Motors revised guidance, especially in terms of free cash flow, is significant and can be credited for the market's strong and positive reaction on Tuesday.

General Motors’ valuation

General Motors continues to trade at what, I believe, is an excessive earnings yield of 19%. The automaker is priced at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 5.4X, which makes General Motors even cheaper than Ford Motor (F).

Ford Motor, which is General Motors' most significant high-volume, ICE-focused rival in the U.S. market, is trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 5.9X. Ford’s valuation implies a similarly impressive earnings yield of 17%. Both Ford and General Motors are still overly focused on their ICE segments, but EV momentum for both companies is pointing upward... which indicates that both companies could see growing EV earnings momentum next year.

General Motors is now trading above its longer term, 3-year P/E average and shares just climbed to a new 1-year high in response to the better than expected Q3 earnings sheet. In my last work on General Motors, I argued that shares could revalue to a 6-7X forward P/E ratio, considering that GM raised its guidance for FY 2024 on strong sales momentum across its auto brands. I believe with a third consecutive guidance raise, General Motors could indeed revalue to a fair value P/E ratio of 6-7X. This multiplier range implies a fair value for General Motors' shares of $60-70 per-share (based off of a consensus estimate of $10.02 per-share for FY 2025).

Risks with General Motors

GM is still overly focused on ICE vehicles, as only about 5% of its sales are electric vehicles. Nonetheless, the EV sales figures for the third-quarter clearly show that the automaker is considerable momentum in its EV division. This indicates to me that the risk profile has improved since my last work on the automaker in July. What would change my mind about General Motors is if the company were to miss its 2024 EV targets of 200,000 units, or if the company failed to see continual momentum in its important EV division.

Final thoughts

Buying the drop after General Motors Q2 results proved to be a very lucrative strategy for investors. General Motors is nearing an important inflection point in its business as well: with volumes in the EV segment surging, the company is looking at a period of strong earnings growth. General Motors’ electric vehicle sales surged 46% Q/Q in Q3'24, indicating that concerns over a slowdown in this segment may be exaggerated. The most important take-away from the Q3 report was that General Motors raised its outlook yet again, especially in terms of free cash flow. With a 19% earnings yield available, I believe shares of General Motors, despite hitting a new 1-year high, are undervalued.

data.title: ServiceNow: Q3, Broaden Now Platform With AI Capabilities
data.description:
I discussed ServiceNow’s (NYSE:NOW) market expansion into mid-office and back-office platforms in my previous ‘Strong Buy’ thesis published in July 2024. With the launch of Xanadu & RaptorDB, ServiceNow has been enhancing their AI offerings and expanding into adjacent market such as database. ServiceNow delivered a strong growth in Q3, beating the marketing expectations and slightly raising the full-year revenue growth guidance. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year price target of $1,100 per share.

Now Platform Xanadu & RaptorDB

In September, ServiceNow released their Now Platform Xanadu, adding more than 350 out-of-the-box gen AI capabilities to Now Assist. These features include data visualization automation, chat- and email- reply generation, change summaries, and LLM-based proactive prompts in Virtual Agent.

In the same month, ServiceNow released their RaptorDB Pro, a high‑performance database enabling customers to centralize operational data and analytics onto ServiceNow platforms. I think these product launches could strengthen ServiceNow’s leadership in business automation and IT service management spaces. Key reasons are:

  • As discussed in my previous reports, ServiceNow has been actively expanding their IT platform into mid-office and back-office. ReptorDB could help ServiceNow generate additional revenue growth from the cloud database market. I don’t expect ServiceNow becoming a database company such as MongoDB (MDB), their database offering could complement their holistic IT platform.
  • Their Xanadu platform enhances ServiceNow’s existing platforms, offering a broad range of AI features. These AI features could potentially further automate customers’ IT workflow and improve productivities. In addition, these AI features are provided as adds-on services, which could generate additional revenue for ServiceNow.

Q3, Outlook and DCF

ServiceNow released its Q3 FY24 result on October 23rd after the market closed, reporting 22% constant revenue growth and raising its full-year revenue growth guidance to 23%, up from the previous guidance of 22%. Impressively, the number of customers with more than $1 million in annual contract value (ACV) increased by 14% year-over-year, while the transactions over $5 million in net new ACV grew by 50% year-over-year. The strong deal growth demonstrated ServiceNow’s relevance and leadership in IT automation and AI platform for business digitalization. During the earnings call, the management expressed strong confidence that their latest Xanadu release will fuel future topline growth and margin expansion.

For growth from FY25 onwards, I am raising the organic revenue growth assumption from 20% to 22% for the following reasons:

  • As the current remaining performance obligations ((cPRO)) has been growing by more than 20% in recent quarters, I think the robust growth in cPRO will gradually turn into actual revenue growth for ServiceNow.
  • Grand View Research predicts that the global information technology service management (ITSM) market will grow at a CAGR of 9.3% from 2023 to 2030. It’s worth noting that ServiceNow has been gaining market share from legacy players, outpacing the overall market growth. Additionally, ServiceNow has expanded their core ITSM into other IT services markets, as discussed previously.
  • I anticipate these new market expansions could contribute an additional 5% growth to ServiceNow. Therefore, the total organic revenue growth is comprised of 9% market growth, 5% new segments growth, and 8% market share gains.
  • I assume the ServiceNow will allocate 6% of total revenue towards acquisitions, contributing 1.5% to topline growth.

I continue to forecast ServiceNow will expand their margin by 250bps annually driven by 100bps from gross profits, 100bps from R&D reductions and 50bps from SG&A leverage. I maintain the cost of equity to be 15% assuming: risk-free rate 3.8%; beta 1.65; equity risk premium 7%. I calculate the future free cash flow from equity (FCFE) as follows:

Discounting all the future FCFE, the one-year target price is calculated to be $1,100 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risks

ServiceNow spent almost 18% of total revenue in stock-based compensations (SBC) in FY23, a quite high percentage compared to other software companies. These stock options have already increased their total number of shares over the past few years, growing approximately 1.5% year-over-year. In Q3 FY24, ServiceNow allocated 15% of total revenue towards SBC, representing 300bps improvement compared to the same quarter last year, which is an encouraging sign. Considering these factors, I model 1% shares dilutions going forward, factoring in the total amount of SBC expenses. The high SBC payout will continue to dilute existing shareholder’s interest in the future.

Conclusion

The Now Platform Xanadu and RaptorDB product launches demonstrate ServiceNow’s capability to expand their core ITSM into other growth areas. With all these IT management platforms, enterprise customers could further leverage AI to automate their IT workflows. I reiterate a ‘Strong Buy’ rating with a one-year price target of $1,100 per share.

data.title: SLM Corporation 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by SLM Corporation in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.
